item.content:encoded: Love this. Really cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically a noclip Katamari in space? +1-ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not the biggest fan of Hard Science Fiction but, to be honest, the execution and writing of this is really well done here. Gets a +1 from me for sure!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to write a strangelet SCP. It's basically what you wrote. I cannot stress just how much i love this and regret having only one upvote to give.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Terrifying and deep. I like that you even slipped some reptilian aliens in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Interesting, but it goes on a bit longer than it needs to.

item.title: A comment on the description of dark matter
item.content:encoded:
I've got a criticism on the science of dark matter in this SCP which I feel is important to mention, but before that let me say I enjoyed this SCP a lot, from the use of detailed particle physics in a way which is very readable to the weirdness of the resulting anomaly, so please don't take this as a negative criticism :)

My criticism is that (from my understanding) part of the basis for this SCP is that a clump of dark matter happens to be near Earth, which is treated as an unusual occurrence.
This is missing or misunderstanding an important point about dark matter as it's currently theorised: that dark matter is distributed in galaxy in a roughly even sphere, and therefore the Earth is expected to be constantly moving through some amount of dark matter (although likely not a large amount, because the sphere of dark matter is not expected to be very dense).

I mention this because it's important from the perspective of scientific education/outreach. Dark matter is one of the central questions in physics at the moment, so getting more understanding out there is fantastic, and this particular concept of the distribution of dark matter is central to understanding why we think dark matter is there in the first place and to how we hope to observe it, and therefore important to include.

I realise there's a challenge in that the concept of this SCP partly relies on the horror of potentially encountering more dark matter, and so including the idea that we're actually passing through dark matter constantly goes against that sting.
I don't have a firm suggestion for what to do differently, but a rough idea could be something like: dark matter particles only enter the phase seen in this SCP after a very high energy collision with other dark matter particles.

Something like that doesn't change most of the SCP, and maintains the horror because this is something that could happen anywhere, albeit very rarely. Therefore we're unlucky that it's occurred so close to Earth, but also it could potentially happen again.

item.title: Crosslink
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Equivalent to a little more than 10 mL of neutronium

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I submit this song as a potential theme song for this anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…So I just came back to this comment, and I realized: the idea I had bubbling in my head back in December 2015 turned into SCP-3966.

Yeah, I sometimes let my ideas sit in my head for a year or two before I use them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I like practically everything about this SCP (hard scifi is my jam), I find the twist that it is dark matter to be pushing against my sense of disbelief too much. Sure, I can understand that there may be something anomalous about how the Earth hasn't encountered dark matter yet, but the fact that the rest of the universe (and solar system) hasn't been chopped up a fair amount is really iffy to me. Also, I feel like 2460 should be a lot more translucent than the photo suggests it is, though I know little about albedo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These electron clouds are negatively charged and push each other away, and that's why we can't walk through walls.

Teeeeechnically it's more to do with the exclusion principle, if memory serves correctly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! It's good to know I managed to make it something the layman could understand. I was afraid at first when my thought experiment turned into quantum mechanics and cosmology, but spelling it out and then immediately adding, "What this means is…" worked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm about as far as it gets from a quantum physicist and hard sci-fi on the subject tends to tie my brain in knots, so I have to seriously commend this article for explaining what's going on and why it's a problem in a way that doesn't require that of the reader.

Even if you removed the addendum that spells it out as dark matter (a conclusion I'd never have come close to), it's still good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn it, WrongJohnSilver, your sci-fi is so hard. Like, rock hard. That is some turgid, tumescent sci-fi right there.

… O.O You did this on purpose, did you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going to have to agree with this comment, love this scip and it's my favorite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

The SCP itself isn't actually scary in itself, but it's the cold, hard science behind it that makes it outstanding.

This is probably my favourite SCP on this wiki as it is plausible, making it terrifying. The connection with the Black Knight Satellite is also a nice touch, as it bonds fiction with reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'm working on the hard science of another one (but I've got so many different ideas in the works it'll be a while…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God damn it, WrongJohnSilver, your sci-fi is so hard. Like, rock hard. That is some turgid, tumescent sci-fi right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In short, ladies and gentlemen, SCP-2460 is dark matter.

whelp that scared me shitless

good work +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wondering what I mean regarding dark matter? Here is another discussion about it!

(ETA: Hat tip to Decibelles for finding it)

item.title: Black Knight!
item.content:encoded: Normally I hate real-world mysteries "explained" with SCPs, but this was a wonderful job - both the Black Knight AND dark matter at once!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm considering adding footnotes, but I'd like to ask you and everyone else, what footnotes would you want to see to make this clearer to you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank thou for quick dealing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Good catch there. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By the way, shouldn't this be tagged "k-class-scenario" and "uncontained"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this would be very exemplary model of "techno SCP".
In fact this is not anomaly. It can be explained by our science and furthermore can be decisive evidence of scientific theory. But it is bloody dangerous and Foundation must get rid of it.
Science fiction, dangerous monster, and priority of Foundation is all in this article. This is god damn fascinating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
Very well written, I am quite fond of the scientific "accuracy" (so to speak) and the link with dark matter was a very nice touch !

A quick thing though : at some point in the past (like, a few billions years ago), the Universe was so dense that ordinary and dark matter were constantly interacting, so I just wanted to say that our Universe is pretty safe from the kind of scenario that you describe :-) (because it would have already happened a long time ago otherwise).

I hope I didn't ruin the SCP for anyone with my remark ! (and I apologise if I did)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooh, WrongJohnSilver, are you a pioneer? ;>

Thank you for the explanation above!

But you know what? That technobabble is what makes this thing anomalous.

Quite. Anomalous AND awesome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second this wholeheartedly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And so, a couple days later, Scientific American talks about dark bosons.

Here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey look! It's a Black Knight skip! +1

It's nice to come across a hard science SCP every once and a while. That said, might do you some good to throw in some footnotes for the laymans. Just remember your audience when you write on certain topics. Not everyone is as well versed in physics as you.

Also, great job at suspending my disbelief on this one and using a real life tie-in. I'm a sucker for skips that are actual urban legends/conspiracies in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very cool article, but why is this anomalous? It seems pretty scientifically explained, is the mechanism for conversion to exotic matter the anomalous part of this? It sounds like you might be suggesting this:

A planetesimal with the above dimensions of 50m x 30m x 15m. This item acts as the "surface" of SCP-2460. Of note is the distinct lack of cratering on the surface.

Is the source of the anomaly, but strictly speaking, if that planetesimal is converting things into exotic matter, the anomalous portion of this is entirely THAT object, not the chunk of dark matter?

Probably nit picky since the angle here was hard science, but there is/was ground to take this even further into awesome territory. This is one of those corner cases where public engagement is possible (the scientific community might not exactly peg this as anomalous, especially considering the way you ended that lecture), and full mobilisation of Earth's resources is warranted, so this could be a pseudo-veil breaker, or a format screw where you can introduce documentation where the Foundation is branching out into arresting "mundane" apocalypses, or even make this an -EX. I mean there is serious 001 proposal potential here.

Either way, excellent work, emphatic +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes. The truth is, there's no risk that this will turn into a black hole. The Earth had a Schwarzschild radius of 9 mm, after all. In this case, it's a literary device to say "this item is super dense" and a chance to scare the reader with, "Will this turn into a black hole?"

But that's why the mandatory 50 km perimeter exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the significance of the Schwarzschild radius here? I'm assuming that that specifically refers to the point at which light cannot escape; wouldn't it be more relevant to calculate the distance at which spacecraft cannot escape from 2460?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pls write more hard sf stuff

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Double yeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeessss

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeesssssssssssssss

All my +1s

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mostly science, actually. Here's what's science:

  • The orbital mechanics for something of that mass were determined mathematically. Since it's still far smaller than the mass of the Earth, its own contributions are nearly negligible.
  • Dark matter is known to be weakly interacting massive particles (often called by the acronym WIMP). By "massive" we don't mean "huge," we mean "has mass." They do not have an electromagnetic interaction, nor does dark matter appear to meaningfully interact with itself. Only gravitation appears to apply to dark matter.
  • The electromagnetic interaction is why we can't walk through walls. Our atoms' electrons push against the walls' electrons, and like charges repel each other (protons are positive, electrons are negative, and opposites attract). So, in dark matter, that repulsion doesn't exist. Thus, dark matter particles can, in essence, pass through each other without impact.
  • Normal matter, made of electrons and quarks (which in turn make protons and neutrons), are fermions, and similar fermionic particles cannot occupy the same quantum state at the same time. This is called the Pauli Exclusion Principle. There are other particles, called bosons, which can - photons, or light particles, being the most well known. And yes, the photon is the gauge boson of the electromagnetic interaction. That means it carries the power of the electromagnetic interaction between subatomic particles.
  • The difference between fermions and bosons is something called spin. Basically, we imagine electrons and quarks and all to be something like a ball. You can spin the ball, and a faster spinning ball will have more energy in it than a slower-spinning ball, but the ball doesn't have to be traveling anywhere. Similarly, particles can carry more or less energy in this way. The thing is, fermions have half-integer spin, while bosons have integer spin. If you want to remove the Pauli Exclusion Principle from matter, you have to change the spin from half-integer to full-integer (and zero is a possible integer).
  • There is a theory of particle physics, called supersymmetry, that posits that every fermionic particle has a bosonic partner. Furthermore, supersymmetry suggests that these bosonic particles are the WIMPs that dark matter is made of.

Now for the technobabble:

  • I made up the term "quantum spin annihilation." However, if we do turn fermions into bosons, then reducing the particles' spin to zero would do the trick. Thus, quantum spin annihilation is a valid term for the phenomenon.
  • In all honesty, matter that had its spin annihilated would, most likely, cease to be objects, and collapse into a state of matter called a Bose-Einstein condensate. Also, without electromagnetic interaction, there's nothing that would necessarily hold it together. But that's no fun.
  • Without electromagnetism, there's no interaction with photons. Thus, the material should be invisible and intangible. But then we've got nothing more than undetectable ghost particles flying through space, and who cares.
  • Although supersymmetry allows for the existence of bosonic matter (with selectrons and squarks instead of electrons and quarks), recent experiments make it seem unlikely that supersymmetry holds.

But you know what? That technobabble is what makes this thing anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would LOVE to know whether this is, indeed, technobabble or real science… (Please don't be mad at me, WrongJohnSilver! ;>)

Anyway, +1. Excellent skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I just…

It's been a while since I saw a real hard-science skip. I feel like you went out of your way to explain things as clearly as possible, but even the explanations need explanations.

I was kind of feeling it, nevertheless, up until "it's dark matter". That seemed to be one step too far up the "super-science scary" ladder. I'm just gonna hold off on this one, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just re-read the first part and noticed that I mistook density for mass. My physics teacher would be proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I calculated the orbital mechanics to make sure it didn't. Also, despite SCP-2460's mass, the Earth's mass is still 1011 times greater. Earth still dwarfs this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't this supermassive object be altering orbiters' orbits continuously?

Yes, but even an object of this mass is going to be completely dwarfed by the mass of the Earth. Furthermore, as a captured object, it can travel in an oblique path so that it almost never encounters other satellites. As an example, when OU11 was 70 km from it, the gravitational acceleration it experienced from it was 1.69 x 10-7 m/s2, so it would take exposure at that distance of 3 minutes before the station moved a millimeter.

Wouldn't the sheer amount of space debris present in orbit, albeit with a lot of empty space around them, separating them, have interacted with the anomaly previously a lot of times?

Yes. Much of the debris would likely either be deflected to fall to Earth, get ejected, or contribute to the mass. However, most debris is low in mass and won't perturb the anomaly considerably. Also, we imagine debris like fictional depictions of the asteroid belt—lots of bits near each other. In truth, all these bits have tens if not hundreds or thousands of kilometers between them. It's still quite sparse, just worth tracking.

Finally, is all this at all measurable with conventional, current technology? Or would the Foundation resort to SCP-derived tech to study it?

Mass, electromagnetic and gravitational interaction, and orbital trajectory? Absolutely. The bosonic nature of the matter? From observation, yes. The quantum spin annihilation would still be a hypothesis, hard to test since you don't want to touch it, but it would be a reasonable interaction that would explain the phasing phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to my physics class I understood most of this, more or less.

This one was a weird one for me. Normally I don't like addendums at the end that tell you everything that is happening, but in this one I welcomed it. It felt better to read it being explained by someone explaining it to a non-physicist audience rather than trying to piece it together. I also really liked the ending.

One question though, if this thing is so massive and passes so close to Earth, why isn't the Earth sucked right into it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to point out how I'm not a physicist, not even a physics connoisseur, so these are ignorant questions.

Wouldn't this supermassive object be altering orbiters' orbits continuously?

Wouldn't the sheer amount of space debris present in orbit, albeit with a lot of empty space around them, separating them, have interacted with the anomaly previously a lot of times?

Finally, is all this at all measurable with conventional, current technology? Or would the Foundation resort to SCP-derived tech to study it?

The transcript at the end is excellent; I'm not sure about the rest, will revisit and decide on a vote. Otherwise, good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how the anomaly here isn't really the fact that this mass of dark matter is so close to Earth, but more so the fact that the Earth itself has survived billions of years without being obliterated by the stuff. I really like that.

Perhaps it didn't need to be spelled out so obviously in the transcript, but regardless of that, this is a great article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat! The crocodillian astronauts swung me a little bit out of it, but excellent overall!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted once. Then I upvoted again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus fuck.

I…Jesus fuck.

Anborough Stamp of Keter Approval hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds like something Stephen Baxter would write about. Interesting but I don't get why objects inside the thing retain any coherent structure. Upvote anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hard science ftw

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm seeing it too. Probably just your computer, Meta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The science is really nice, and QK-Class end-of-the-world scenario is one of the most unique end-of-the-world scenarios I've ever seen on this site.

Overall, I can see how you've thoroughly through how a large mass of dark matter would act like (the varying shape, the QK-Class scenario as well as its electromagnetic properties), and I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can also see the image. It may be a problem on your end rather than the coding's end.

That being said, this is a great SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still not working. Hopefully someone more experienced can help you. I've had the same problem where I could *see* my images, but others could not. Think it has to do with hosting. I'm bad with technical things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how you made the black night satellite an SCP, and you did it well!! Up-vote from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I do feel like the note at the end almost too abruptly forces what you're trying to do on the reader, the rest of it is an excellent balance of technobabble and storytelling and the concept is cool enough to upvote.

Here's hoping for more QK-Class scenarios into future articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! It should work now (at least, it's working on my computer).
